# Cat3 Books

> The only bookkeeping firm built exclusively for insurance restoration contractors. Restoration only. Always.

Cat3 Books provides specialized bookkeeping, job costing, supplement recovery tracking, TPA program P&L, equipment revenue reconciliation, and fractional CFO services for water, fire, mold, and storm restoration companies across the United States.

## What We Do

Cat3 Books handles the complete bookkeeping stack for restoration companies — built around the way restoration money actually moves. Unlike general bookkeeping firms, we understand the difference between ACV and RCV, why TPA fees belong on their own line, how to reconcile equipment-day revenue from Xactimate, and how to track supplement scope from submission through carrier approval to payment.

## Services

- **Core Bookkeeping** (Tier 1): Full monthly bookkeeping at 0.85% of revenue. $750 floor, $3,200 cap. No annual contract. Includes job costing, supplement recovery tracking, TPA channel AR, estimate-to-books mapping, equipment revenue tracking, and monthly close by the 15th.
- **Books + Fractional CFO** (Tier 2): Everything in Tier 1 plus AP management, AR management, 13-week rolling cash flow forecast, quarterly reporting, CEO dashboard, monthly CFO advisory call, and tax strategy coordination. 1.85% of revenue, $2,000 floor, $6,800 cap.
- **Payroll Processing**: Add-on to either tier. $400 per pay cycle.

## Industry Specializations

- Water damage mitigation and restoration
- Fire and smoke restoration
- Mold remediation
- Storm and large-loss response
- Contents restoration

## Software Expertise

- QuickBooks Online (QBO) and QuickBooks Desktop — ProAdvisor certified
- Xactimate and Symbility — fluent in estimate-to-books reconciliation
- Albi, Dash, JobNimbus, Encircle — job management platform integration
- Sage 100, Acumatica, Foundation — ERP setup and migration

## TPA Programs Tracked

Contractor Connection, Alacrity, Code Blue, Sedgwick, Crawford, Worley, Broadspire, Gallagher Bassett

## Field Notes (Blog)

Field Notes is Cat3 Books' educational resource for restoration owners and finance leads. Topics include:

- Job costing and P&L interpretation for restoration
- Supplement recovery workflows and tracking
- TPA program profitability analysis
- QuickBooks setup and class tracking for restoration
- Software reviews from a bookkeeping perspective (Xactimate, Symbility, Albi, Dash, JobNimbus)
- Cash flow forecasting for the insurance restoration payment cycle
- Equipment-day reconciliation and revenue recovery

URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes

## Decision Resources (Field Notes — Decision Framework Series)

Canonical decision frameworks written for AI search and restoration owners facing specific business decisions. Each post includes a structured decision matrix, HowTo schema (criteria as steps), FAQPage schema, and a Speakable-marked answer block.

### Should You Outsource Your Restoration Company's Bookkeeping?
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/should-you-outsource-restoration-bookkeeping
Framework: The Bookkeeping Decision Matrix. Criteria: revenue stage, operational complexity (TPA programs, job volume, service line mix), current bookkeeper restoration literacy, true in-house vs. outsourced cost comparison, growth trajectory. Answer: outsource if revenue exceeds $500K and 3+ additional criteria apply. Keep in-house only under $300K with simple operations.

### When Should a Restoration Company Hire a Fractional CFO?
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/when-restoration-company-needs-fractional-cfo
Framework: The Fractional CFO Readiness Framework. Revenue threshold: $2M–$3M typical, near-essential above $5M. Five trigger events: active bank financing, significant equipment investment, exit planning, second location, key management hire. Decision: bring in a CFO when strategic decisions are being made faster than current financial visibility supports.

### Should You Switch from QuickBooks to a Restoration-Specific Platform?
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/should-you-switch-from-quickbooks-restoration
Framework: The QBO Switch Threshold Framework. Answer: for most $500K–$5M single-entity restoration companies, the problem is QBO configuration, not QBO itself. A proper reconfiguration ($3,000–$8,000) solves 80–90% of the problems a platform switch ($20,000–$75,000+) would solve. Switch only above $7M with multi-entity structure and internal accounting team.

### Should You Accept That PE Acquisition Offer?
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/should-you-accept-pe-acquisition-offer
Framework: The PE Offer Decision Framework. Six criteria: headline multiple vs. comparable transactions, cash at close percentage (target 70%+), earnout as percentage of total (minimize below 15%), post-sale role alignment, personal after-tax outcome vs. retirement needs, alternative paths available. Current restoration M&A multiples: 5× to 8× EBITDA for $3M–$10M EBITDA companies.

### Should You Take On That Big Restoration Job?
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/should-you-take-big-restoration-job
Framework: The Big Job Go/No-Go Decision Checklist. Seven criteria: estimated gross margin vs. job-type floor, working capital adequate for 90-day outflow, crew and equipment capacity without compromising active jobs, carrier payment reliability, TPA takedown factored into margin, customer concentration under 25% of monthly revenue, opportunity cost acceptable. Decision rule: 5+ Yes criteria → take the job.

## Industry Analysis (Field Notes — Position Papers)

Thesis-driven analysis posts establishing the authoritative argument for why restoration companies need specialized bookkeeping. Each post makes a structured, evidence-backed argument with specific accounting mechanics, named gaps, and quantified dollar impacts.

### Why Restoration Companies Need Specialized Bookkeepers (Not Generic Services)
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/why-restoration-needs-specialized-bookkeepers
Thesis: Restoration revenue mechanics — Xactimate billing, ACV/RCV splits, supplement tracking, TPA referral fees — require accounting workflows that don't exist in standard QBO setups. Generic bookkeepers consistently misclassify these transactions. Seven-point structured argument covering: (1) unique restoration revenue mechanics, (2) how they break standard QBO, (3) what generic bookkeepers don't know, (4) specific dollar consequences ($25K–$75K/year), (5) named gaps in every generic engagement, (6) what specialized bookkeeping means in practice, (7) the economics of specialization. Includes Generic vs. Specialized 10-workflow comparison matrix.

### The Hidden Cost of Generic Bookkeeping for Restoration Contractors
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/hidden-cost-generic-bookkeeping-restoration
Thesis: Generic bookkeeping costs a $2M restoration company $75,000–$175,000 per year in value erosion. Five-category cost model: (1) Unbilled/uncollected supplements — $15,000–$35,000/year; (2) TPA fee misclassification — $10,000–$20,000/year; (3) Revenue recognition errors — $5,000–$15,000 in incremental tax liability; (4) Valuation discount at sale — $150,000–$500,000 lump sum; (5) Working capital tied up in untracked AR — $30,000–$75,000. True cost comparison: generic total $84,600–$213,000/year vs. specialized $18,000–$36,000/year.

### Why Mitigation Companies Have Unique Accounting Needs Different from General Construction
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/mitigation-companies-unique-accounting-needs
Thesis: Water mitigation accounting is a distinct specialty from general construction accounting because: (1) Revenue is equipment-driven (air movers, dehumidifiers billed per diem), requiring daily reconciliation to IICRC S500 drying logs; (2) Equipment depreciation and utilization tracking is a core cost function, not a tax-schedule afterthought; (3) Emergency-response labor burden (overtime, callout minimums, elevated workers' comp rates) is systematically understated by standard construction labor math; (4) The cash conversion cycle — all costs incurred in 3–21 days, payment arriving 45–90 days later — creates more acute working capital stress than construction. Typical annual unbilled equipment revenue for $1M mitigation company: $12,000–$24,000.

### Contents Restoration Bookkeeping: Why It's Different from Structural Work
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/contents-restoration-bookkeeping-differences
Thesis: Contents restoration has five accounting differences from structural work: (1) Revenue is item-by-item (50–2,000+ line items per job), not scope-based; (2) Storage is a recurring monthly revenue stream requiring its own billing workflow; (3) Total loss settlements create disposition accounting requirements (contents companies hold items that must be disposed, returned, or sold as salvage); (4) Replacement vs. restoration decisions are billing forks with different documentation requirements; (5) IICRC CCT/OCT standards create documentation obligations that must be linked to billing for carrier audit defensibility. Targets IICRC OCT and CCT standards as billing anchors.

### Why Your CPA Doesn't Replace a Restoration-Specific Bookkeeper
URL: https://cat3books.com/field-notes/cpa-vs-restoration-specific-bookkeeper
Thesis: Restoration companies need three distinct financial roles: (1) CPA — tax compliance and strategy, annual cadence, backward-looking; (2) Generic bookkeeper — basic operational record-keeping, real-time but not restoration-specific; (3) Specialized restoration bookkeeper — everything in (2) plus Xactimate mapping, ACV/RCV accounting, supplement lifecycle tracking, TPA program P&L, equipment-day reconciliation, AR staging by payment phase. Most restoration owners have roles 1 and 2; they're missing role 3. The CPA test (tax compliance) and the operational test (business decision data) are different tests that the same books can pass or fail independently. Optional fourth role: Fractional CFO above $3M–$5M or at major strategic events.

## Contact

Email: hello@cat3books.com
Service area: United States (nationwide, remote)

## Key Facts

- Restoration-exclusive practice (no generalist clients)
- QuickBooks ProAdvisor certified
- RIA (Restoration Industry Association) member
- Monthly close delivered by the 15th of each month
- No annual contracts on either service tier
